nativeRange.habitat: The Purple Firefish inhabits deep outer reef slopes and reef-base hard bottoms with moderate to strong currents, typically over patches of sand and rubble close to shelter. Adults are commonly observed in pairs hovering just above the substrate, picking off zooplankton carried by the current.

diet.notes: A planktivore (a species that feeds primarily on plankton) in the wild, the Purple Firefish picks off zooplankton, copepods, and crustacean larvae drifting in the current. In captivity it has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and prepared frozen carnivore diets. Offering 2–3 small feedings per day is generally advised; the vibrancy of the purple and violet fin coloration tends to decline on a low-quality or vitamin-poor diet.

avoidWith: other Purple Firefish (unless an established mated pair)

summary: The Purple Firefish is a small deep-water dartfish with a pale body offset by vivid purple-and-yellow fins, generally considered beginner-friendly in peaceful reef aquariums but notably more conspecific-aggressive than the related common Firefish (N. magnifica).
careNotes:
The Purple Firefish (Nemateleotris decora) is a dartfish in the family Microdesmidae, belonging to the subfamily Ptereleotrinae, and is not a true goby — although it is closely related to gobies in the order Gobiiformes, which is why the common name 'Purple Firefish Goby' is technically a misnomer. It is broadly regarded as a beginner-friendly reef fish: generally hardy, peaceful toward other species, and reef-safe with no documented coral or invertebrate nipping.

A tightly-sealed lid is essential. Like other dartfish, this species is a strong jumper and has been widely reported leaping from open-top tanks when startled. Most losses of this species in captivity are reportedly attributable to jumping rather than to disease or aggression.

The Purple Firefish tends to be more conspecific-aggressive (aggressive toward its own kind) than the related common Firefish (N. magnifica) and is commonly considered more territorial within the genus. It is best kept singly or as a confirmed male–female mated pair. Two unrelated specimens in the same tank tend to fight, often with fatal consequences for the subordinate fish.

A minimum tank of around 75 L (approximately 20 US gal) is commonly recommended; some sources consider a 38 L (10 US gal) nano adequate for a single individual, but a 75 L aquarium gives more swimming room and dilutes territorial pressure. Provide ample loose rubble and rockwork with crevices so the fish can dart into shelter — an individual without a bolt-hole is more likely to jump when stressed.

In the wild this is a notably deep-water species, recorded from about 25 m down to 100 m (typically 25–70 m) on outer reef slopes from Mauritius east to Samoa, north to the Ryukyu Islands, south to New Caledonia. Although most collected specimens come from depths above the strict mesophotic zone, deeper-caught individuals can carry some decompression stress; quarantine and slow acclimation are generally advised.

Reported lifespan is around 3 years, with 5 years considered achievable on a high-quality, varied diet.
breedingNotes: Reported as monogamous in the wild, with bonded pairs commonly seen sharing a sand/rubble burrow. Captive breeding has not been widely documented and is considered difficult, primarily because the planktonic larvae are extremely small and require live first-feed cultures.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Confirmed 20-gal minimum, tight-fitting lid required (jumper), conspecific aggression unless mated pair, reef-safe, peaceful toward other species, diet of frozen mysis/brine shrimp + chopped seafood. Also captures the synonyms 'Decorated Firefish', 'Purple Dartfish', 'Decorated Dartfish'.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Microdesmidae, subfamily Ptereleotrinae, order Gobiiformes), max size (9.0 cm TL), native range (Mauritius to Samoa, north to Ryukyu Is., south to New Caledonia), habitat (deep outer reef drop-offs, hard bottoms with sand/rubble patches), depth range (25–100 m, usually 25–70 m), water temperature (23–27°C), and monogamous mating system. IUCN: Least Concern.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~10 cm / 4 in), care level (easy), temperament (peaceful toward other species, highly conspecific-aggressive), reef-safe (yes), diet (planktivorous carnivore — copepods/zooplankton/mysis/brine), lifespan (~3 years), water parameters (72–80°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), and jumping behavior requiring a tight lid. Source incorrectly cites family Gobiidae; FishBase's Microdesmidae was preferred.

additional.name: Aquariadise
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (20 gal recommended; 10 gal floor), temperament (peaceful with other species, aggressive to own kind — house singly or as mated pair only, per Scott Michael), lifespan (3–5 years with quality diet), and jumping behavior. Aquariadise was used in lieu of Reef2Reef wiki because the wiki has no species page for N. decora.

fish.breedingNotes: Monogamous pairs form naturally, and eggs are reportedly deposited in or near a sand or rubble burrow. Larvae are planktonic and very small, making first-feed rearing the primary bottleneck. No widely-replicated captive-breeding protocol has been documented in the hobby.
